primary_diagnosis.name: Critical limb ischaemia with an arterial (ischaemic) leg ulcer

pathophysiology_summary: Chronic peripheral arterial disease has progressed to critical limb ischaemia: the classic triad of ischaemic rest pain (worse supine/on elevation, relieved by dependency as gravity aids perfusion), tissue loss (a punched-out, sharply demarcated ankle ulcer with dry black eschar and no granulation), and absent distal perfusion (cold, pale, shiny, hairless limb; absent dorsalis pedis/posterior tibial pulses; capillary refill >5 s). Discriminators: a venous ulcer is shallow, sloped, over the gaiter area with haemosiderin and oedema and is relieved by elevation — the opposite here; a neuropathic diabetic ulcer is typically painless with a warm well-perfused foot; infected ulcer/cellulitis would show heat, spreading erythema, pus and fever, which are absent. Management is urgent revascularisation assessment and analgesia; crucially the leg must NOT be elevated or compressed (would worsen ischaemia). Acute-on-chronic deterioration (suddenly blue/black toes, numb paralysed foot) or sepsis are limb/life-threatening emergencies.

differential.evidence_basis: Rest pain relieved by dependency, cold pale foot, punched-out black-based ankle ulcer.
'Leg ulcer' framing invites this, but punched-out morphology, pain on elevation and absent pulses argue strongly against it — and compression/elevation would be harmful.
Neuropathic ulcers are typically painless with a warm foot — exquisite pain and a cold foot argue against.
Necrotic wound could superinfect; screen for fever/spread/pus.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Recognise ARTERIAL ulcer / CLI and explicitly avoid elevation/compression; escalate urgently. Anchoring on venous ulcer and advising compression is a critical error.

differential.evidence_basis: CLI triad confirmed on history; limb-threatening, needs urgent vascular assessment.
Punched-out morphology, elevation-worsened pain, absent pulses exclude venous aetiology.
Afebrile, no spreading erythema or pus — but remains a safety-net.

differential.diagnosis: Critical limb ischaemia — limb at risk
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis: urgent vascular/ED assessment, analgesia, keep leg dependent and warm, wound dry; do not elevate or compress.

red_flags.description: Ischaemic rest pain relieved only by dependency
red_flags.clinical_significance: Hallmark of critical limb ischaemia — limb-threatening; mandates urgent revascularisation assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delay risks irreversible ischaemia and major amputation.

red_flags.description: Cold, pale, pulseless foot with black necrotic ulcer base
red_flags.clinical_significance: Absent perfusion and tissue loss — the limb is critically ischaemic.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to gangrene and limb loss.

red_flags.description: Acute limb ischaemia (sudden blue/black toes, numb/paralysed foot) or sepsis (fever, spreading redness)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Acute-on-chronic ischaemia or wound sepsis is an immediate emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Rapid limb loss or life-threatening sepsis.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating a 'leg ulcer' as venous and advising compression/elevation, which worsens arterial ischaemia.
Arranging only routine wound care/outpatient review for a limb-threatening critical ischaemia.
Leaving grossly inadequate pain relief for severe ischaemic rest pain.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent same-day in-person assessment (ED or acute vascular service) for analgesia and revascularisation work-up; ambulance if acute limb ischaemia or sepsis features. Keep the leg dependent and warm; do NOT elevate or compress; keep the wound dry.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Mislabelling as a venous ulcer and advising compression/elevation, or arranging only routine follow-up, risks limb loss.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Critical limb ischaemia (rest pain, tissue loss, absent pulses) requires urgent vascular referral for revascularisation; avoid limb elevation and compression; provide adequate analgesia and cardiovascular risk management.
